response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of the proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation across five random seeds for each configuration. The baseline model (variant_03), which does not include the proposed component, achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6%. This serves as the primary reference point for assessing the impact of the proposed modifications.

Among the nine variants incorporating the proposed method, only variant_08 surpassed the baseline, attaining a mean accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5%. This constitutes an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline, indicating that this particular configuration of the method has a substantial positive effect on model performance. The standard deviation for variant_08 was also lower than that of the baseline, suggesting increased stability across different random seeds.

The remaining eight variants, however, did not outperform the baseline. Specifically, their validation accuracies were as follows: variant_01 achieved 67.2 ± 0.8%, variant_02 66.9 ± 0.7%, variant_04 65.1 ± 0.9%, variant_05 66.3 ± 1.0%, variant_06 64.7 ± 0.7%, variant_07 65.8 ± 0.8%, variant_09 66.5 ± 0.7%, and variant_10 65.9 ± 0.8%. In all these cases, performance was either comparable to or inferior to the baseline, with variant_06 exhibiting the largest drop (−3.1%).

Overall, these results indicate that the effectiveness of the proposed method is highly sensitive to configuration choices. Only one out of nine experimental variants incorporating the proposed component yielded a clear benefit, while the majority did not improve upon the baseline. This highlights the importance of careful selection and ablation of design choices when adapting the proposed method.
